abstract: This paper examines the complex landscape of internationalization in higher education, focusing on emergent tensions between the tradition of education as a public good and its growing status as a commercial export. Through an analysis of the General Agreement on Trade in Services (GATS) and regional case studies—most notably Australia’s heavy reliance on international student revenue—the text explores how "cross-border" education impacts equity, access, and cultural diversity. While market-driven expansion has provided vital subsidies for public institutions, it has also led to concerns regarding the homogenization of curricula, the erosion of domestic quality, and the rise of a "for-profit" sector that often blurs the lines between academic scholarship and vocational training. Ultimately, the paper argues that as international higher education matures, stakeholders must adopt a more critical proactive stance to ensure that the drive for innovation and revenue does not undermine the fundamental quality and cultural integrity of global academic systems.
